findings.title: sse/http cases duplicated in safeRowToConfig, buildConfig, validateConfig
findings.description: The 'sse' and 'http' transport cases are structurally identical across three functions: safeRowToConfig (lines 195-208), buildConfig (lines 243-245), and validateConfig (lines 275-288). In each function the two cases produce the same logic with only the transport literal differing. This amounts to ~20 duplicated lines spread across the file. Since both SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig share the same fields (url + headers), they can be collapsed into fall-through cases or a shared helper.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE (safeRowToConfig):
case 'sse':
  return { ...base, transport: 'sse', url: row.url ?? '', headers: safeJsonParse(...) }
case 'http':
  return { ...base, transport: 'http', url: row.url ?? '', headers: safeJsonParse(...) }

AFTER:
case 'sse':
case 'http':
  return { ...base, transport: row.transport, url: row.url ?? '', headers: safeJsonParse<Record<string, string>>(row.headers, undefined, `headers for server ${row.id}`) } as McpServerConfig

WHY: Reduces duplication across 3 functions. The transport-factory.ts already uses this fall-through pattern (lines 18-19) proving the approach is idiomatic in this codebase.

findings.title: SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig types are structurally identical
findings.description: SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig (and their Input counterparts) have identical fields aside from the transport discriminant literal. This structural duplication is the root cause of the repeated switch-case duplication in server-store.ts. A shared generic or base interface for URL-based transports would remove the need for separate cases throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Verbose type assertions for transport cleanup in catch block
findings.description: The transport cleanup in the catch block uses three chained conditions and two type assertions to call close(). This is a single complex expression spanning the full width of the line. While functionally correct, the repeated type assertions reduce readability.

findings.title: aggregateTools collision logic is non-obvious without comment
findings.description: The collision-handling strategy in aggregateTools is subtle: if a toolName already exists, the new tool is stored under a namespaced key, but the original tool (from a different server) retains the un-namespaced name. This asymmetry is non-obvious -- a reader might expect both the original and the duplicate to be namespaced on collision. A descriptive comment or a clearer variable name would help.

findings.title: validateConfig uses 'in' checks unnecessarily for discriminated union
findings.description: validateConfig accepts McpServerConfigInput | McpServerConfig. Inside the switch on input.transport, TypeScript already narrows the union to the stdio variant, which is guaranteed to have 'command' and optional 'args'. The 'command' in input and 'args' in input guards are redundant since the discriminant has already narrowed the type. Similarly, the 'url' in input checks in the sse/http cases are redundant.

positive_observations: Functions are consistently short (all under 30 lines) with early returns and guard clauses
Clean discriminated union type design in types.ts enables exhaustive switch-case checks
transport-factory.ts already demonstrates the sse/http fall-through pattern showing awareness of the duplication
Good use of named constants (ALLOWED_COMMANDS, SHELL_METACHAR_PATTERN, VALID_TRANSPORTS) -- no magic values
Separation between store (persistence), client manager (connections), and hub (orchestration) follows functional core / imperative shell well
